All committed to `chore/refresh-profile-photos` (now 4 commits). Summary:

- Replaced all 6 blog image masters with your new versions (same filenames, dropped into `themes/portio/assets/images/writing/`).
- Most auto-cropped fine with the existing smart-crop pipeline, but two needed manual anchor overrides after visual review:

- Had to extend the anchor override into the single-post banner template too (I'd only wired it into the thumbnail templates last time).

content.text:
Found it, and it's good news — this isn't from the last PR at all.

**Root cause:** Cloudflare's **Rocket Loader** (a "Speed" optimization feature enabled at the zone level for `coaching.begbie.com`) rewrites our `<script defer>` tag's `type` attribute into a mangled non-JS value (`type="614032a4c9b99e7b97f67c52-text/javascript"`) so it can lazy-load it through its own async loader instead. Browsers refuse to execute a script whose `type` isn't a recognized JS MIME type, so the entire bundle — navbar toggle, testimonial carousel, video popup, contact form — silently never runs. I confirmed this by diffing production's raw HTML against the preview URL's: identical everywhere except that one attribute, and production's response includes an injected `rocket-loader.min.js` script that preview's doesn't (preview URLs on `*.pages.dev` don't go through that DNS zone's proxy settings, so Rocket Loader never touches them).

This means the bug has likely been there since the vanilla-JS rewrite (Phase 2) — Rocket Loader doesn't play well with our `DOMContentLoaded`-based init pattern — and nobody happened to test the live production site's interactive JS since then until now. Nothing in the photo-refresh PR touched JS at all.

**Fix:** add `data-cfasync="false"` to the bundle's `<script>` tag — Cloudflare's documented opt-out that tells Rocket Loader to leave a script alone. One-line change in `footer.html`.

Want me to make that change now on a new branch, or would you rather just disable Rocket Loader for the zone in the Cloudflare dashboard instead? The code fix is more robust (works regardless of dashboard settings), but the dashboard toggle is arguably simpler if you don't want Rocket Loader touching anything else either.
Yes, do that now.  Also, change the footer link to Twitter to instead point to my Bluesky profile (https://bsky.app/profile/rod.begbie.com) with the appropriate icon instead of the Twitter one.

## Summary
- Fixes the mobile-menu-toggle and testimonial-carousel breakage reported in production: Cloudflare's **Rocket Loader** (a Speed optimization enabled on the `coaching.begbie.com` zone) rewrites our `<script>` tag's `type` attribute to a non-JS value so it can lazy-load the script itself. This silently prevents the browser from ever executing it, breaking every `DOMContentLoaded`-gated init (navbar toggle, testimonial carousel, video popup, contact form). Preview deployments on `*.pages.dev` aren't proxied through that zone, so they were unaffected — which is why it only showed up in production.
  - Confirmed root cause by diffing production's raw HTML against a preview deployment's: byte-identical except for an injected `rocket-loader.min.js` script and the mangled `type` attribute on our bundle's `<script>` tag.
  - Fixed with `data-cfasync="false"`, Cloudflare's documented opt-out for Rocket Loader.
  - Not a regression from the previous PR — nothing in that PR touched JS. This has likely been broken in production since the vanilla-JS rewrite, just not noticed until now.
- Replaces the footer's Twitter/X link with a Bluesky profile link (`https://bsky.app/profile/rod.begbie.com`), using Font Awesome's `bluesky` brand glyph in place of the retired `fa-twitter-square` icon.

## Test plan
- [x] `hugo --quiet` production build succeeds
- [x] Verified locally that the bundle script tag now carries `data-cfasync="false"`
- [x] Verified the footer social link points at the Bluesky profile with the new icon rendering correctly (18x18 white SVG, correct href)
- [ ] Verify on the live preview/production deploy that the navbar toggle and testimonial carousel actually work now that Rocket Loader is opted out (can't be verified locally since Rocket Loader only runs on the proxied production domain)

# Frontend Design

Approach this as the design lead at a small studio known for giving every client a visual identity that could not be mistaken for anyone else's. This client has already rejected proposals that felt templated, and is paying for a distinctive point of view: make deliberate, opinionated choices about palette, typography, and layout that are specific to this brief, and take one real aesthetic risk you can justify.

## Ground it in the subject

If the brief does not pin down what the product or subject is, pin it yourself before designing: name one concrete subject, its audience, and the page's single job, and state your choice. If there's any information in your memory about the human's preferences, context about what they're building, or designs you've made before – use that as a hint. The subject's own world, its materials, instruments, artifacts, and vernacular, is where distinctive choices come from. Build with the brief's real content and subject matter throughout.

## Design principles

For web designs, the hero is a thesis. Open with the most characteristic thing in the subject's world, in whatever form makes sense for it: a headline, an image, an animation, a live demo, an interactive moment. Be deliberate with your choice: a big number with a small label, supporting stats, and a gradient accent is the template answer, only use if that's truly the best option.

Typography carries the personality of the page. Pair the display and body faces deliberately, not the same families you would reach for on any other project, and set a clear type scale with intentional weights, widths, and spacing. Make the type treatment itself a memorable part of the design, not a neutral delivery vehicle for the content.

Structure is information. Structural devices, numbering, eyebrows, dividers, labels, should encode something true about the content, not decorate it. Many generic designs use numbered markers (01 / 02 / 03), but that's only appropriate if the content actually is a sequence - like a real process or a typed timeline where order carries information the reader needs. Question if choices like numbered markers actually make sense before incorporating them.

Leverage motion deliberately. Think about where and if animation can serve the subject: a page-load sequence, a scroll-triggered reveal, hover micro-interactions, ambient atmosphere. An orchestrated moment usually lands harder than scattered effects; choose what the direction calls for. However, sometimes less is more, and extra animation contributes to the feeling that the design is AI-generated.

Match complexity to the vision. Maximalist directions need elaborate execution; minimal directions need precision in spacing, type, and detail. Elegance is executing the chosen vision well.

Consider written content carefully. Often a design brief may not contain real content, and it's up to you to come up with copy. Copy can make a design feel as templated as the design itself. See the below section on writing for more guidance.

## Process: brainstorm, explore, plan, critique, build, critique again

For calibration: AI-generated design right now clusters around three looks: (1) a warm cream background (near #F4F1EA) with a high-contrast serif display and a terracotta accent; (2) a near-black background with a single bright acid-green or vermilion accent; (3) a broadsheet-style layout with hairline rules, zero border-radius, and dense newspaper-like columns. All three are legitimate for some briefs, but they are defaults rather than choices, and they appear regardless of subject. Where the brief pins down a visual direction, follow it exactly — the brief's own words always win, including when it asks for one of these looks. Where it leaves an axis free, don't spend that freedom on one of these defaults. Just like a human designer who's hired, there's often a careful balance between doing what you're good at and taking each project as a chance to experiment and learn.

Work in two passes. First, brainstorm a short design plan based on the human's design brief: create a compact token system with color, type, layout, and signature. Color: describe the palette as 4–6 named hex values. Type: the typefaces for 2+ roles (a characterful display face that's used with restraint, a complementary body face, and a utility face for captions or data if needed). Layout: a layout concept, using one-sentence prose descriptions and ASCII wireframes to ideate and compare. Signature: the single unique element this page will be remembered by that embodies the brief in an appropriate way.

Then review that plan against the brief before building: if any part of it reads like the generic default you would produce for any similar page (work through a similar prompt to see if you arrive somewhere similar) rather than a choice made for this specific brief — revise that part, say what you changed and why. Only after you've confirmed the relative uniqueness of your design plan should you start to write the code, following the revised plan exactly and deriving every color and type decision from it.

When writing the code, be careful of structuring your CSS selector specificities. It's easy to generate CSS classes that cancel each other out (especially with a type-based selector like .section and a element-based selector like .cta). This can happen often with paddings/margins between sections.

Try to do a lot of this planning and iteration in your thinking, and only show ideas to the user when you have higher confidence it'll delight them.

## Restraint and self-critique

Spend your boldness in one place. Let the signature element be the one memorable thing, keep everything around it quiet and disciplined, and cut any decoration that does not serve the brief. Not taking a risk can be a risk itself! Build to a quality floor without announcing it: responsive down to mobile, visible keyboard focus, reduced motion respected. Critique your own work as you build, taking screenshots if your environment supports it – a picture is worth 1000 tokens. Consider Chanel's advice: before leaving the house, take a look in the mirror and remove one accessory. Human creators have memory and always try to do something new, so if you have a space to quickly jot down notes about what you've tried, it can help you in future passes.

## More on writing in design

Words appear in a design for one reason: to make it easier to understand, and therefore easier to use. They are design material, not decoration. Bring the same intentionality to copy that you would bring to spacing and color. Before writing anything, ask what the design needs to say, and how it can best be said to help the person navigate the experience.

Write from the end user's side of the screen. Name things by what people control and recognize, never by how the system is built. A person manages notifications, not webhook config. Describe what something does in plain terms rather than selling it. Being specific is always better than being clever.

Use active voice as default. A control should say exactly what happens when it's used: "Save changes," not "Submit." An action keeps the same name through the whole flow, so the button that says "Publish" produces a toast that says "Published." The vocabulary of an interface is the signposting for someone navigating the product. Cohesion and consistency are how people learn their way around.

Treat failure and emptiness as moments for direction, not mood. Explain what went wrong and how to fix it, in the interface's voice rather than a person's. Errors don't apologize, and they are never vague about what happened. An empty screen is an invitation to act.

Keep the register conversational and tuned: plain verbs, sentence case, no filler, with tone matched to the brand and the audience. Let each element do exactly one job. A label labels, an example demonstrates, and nothing quietly does double duty.

# Progress Ledger: Bootstrap 5.3 Upgrade

Plan: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-01-bootstrap-5-upgrade.md

PR: #7 (draft, opened early for Cloudflare preview access)

Baseline commit before Task 1: 7f5da6a

Tasks:
- Task 1: complete (commit 7f5da6a..98a428f, review clean — one-line
  wrangler.jsonc change matches brief exactly. Reviewer independently
  fetched the live preview's raw HTML (cache-busted) and confirmed
  <meta name="generator" content="Hugo 0.163.3"> directly, plus
  cross-checked via gh pr view that the Cloudflare bot comment's
  "Latest commit" matches this exact commit hash with Deploy successful,
  confirming the checked page is genuinely this commit's build, not a
  stale cache).
- Task 2: complete (commit 98a428f..3fb2279, then fix commit 9af39ed —
  review clean after fix). Vendored bootstrap-5.3.8/scss/ correctly
  replacing 4.5.2, import list matches brief byte-for-byte including
  the utilities/api split. Implementer found and correctly fixed a real
  Sass build error not anticipated by the plan: $yiq-text-light
  (BS4 YIQ system) removed in BS5, replaced with $color-contrast-light
  in _typography.scss - reviewer independently grepped the vendored
  source to confirm this is the correct replacement (not hallucinated),
  and swept the whole theme SCSS tree for any other now-undefined BS4
  variables (none found; $link-hover-decoration, the plan's flagged
  risk, does still exist unchanged).
  CRITICAL FOUND AND FIXED: reviewer's independent live-preview check
  caught a real regression the implementer's own report had missed -
  résumé section cards invisible (BS5 dropped grid columns' default
  position:relative, which .resume__education_item implicitly relied
  on to paint above .resume__background). I diagnosed the exact CSS
  stacking-order mechanism myself (systematic-debugging) and fixed
  directly: added position:relative to .resume__education_item
  (commit 9af39ed), matching the pattern already used by hero/about/
  footer sections. Confirmed via independent audit that no other
  section shares this bug (all others already set position explicitly
  on their own elements, not relying on Bootstrap's grid default).
  Re-review on the live preview confirmed the fix with no side effects
  and no regression to anything previously passing.
  Minor (not blocking): vendored bootstrap-5.3.8/scss/tests/ (52KB,
  Bootstrap's own sass-true unit tests) is inert dead weight, same
  category as the dist/js/site/build/nuget bloat trimmed from the old
  4.5.2 vendor tree, just far smaller in scale - worth a future cleanup
  pass, not blocking.
- Task 3: complete (commit 9af39ed..6253226, review clean — matches
  brief exactly across all 6 files. Reviewer independently measured the
  contact form gutter via getBoundingClientRect (24px, correct math:
  16px pe-3 + 8px half of gx-3's 1rem gutter), click-tested the mobile
  nav toggle twice (genuinely opens/closes, all 3 state changes
  verified via DOM, zero console messages during the interaction, with
  console-capture pipeline itself verified functional via a probe),
  confirmed portfolio/single.html and skillSection.html renames correct
  via diff (unreachable, same Phase 1 precedent), and did a full
  repo-wide sweep for any remaining BS4-only classes/attributes -
  none found; two apparent grep hits were both confirmed-dead
  commented-out markup (the example contact form, the hidden résumé
  tab) that don't ship to rendered output). All 3 tasks complete.
  (Note: first dispatch of this review hit a session/usage limit
  mid-way with no verdict; re-dispatched from scratch successfully.)

Final whole-branch review (7f5da6a..03314cc): Ready to merge = Yes.
No Critical/Important issues remaining. Independent verification of the
two mid-implementation fixes ($yiq-text-light->$color-contrast-light,
resume card position:relative) both confirmed correct and sufficiently
targeted; swept every section's SCSS for similar silent Bootstrap-4-
default dependencies and found the resume bug was genuinely unique
(other sections' background layers are thin non-overlapping shapes or
already use explicit z-index:-1).
SECOND REAL FINDING (caught by final review, fixed and re-verified):
contact form's vertical field spacing silently shrank 30px->16px -
Task 3's .form-group->.mb-3 migration (matching Bootstrap's own
migration guide) lost the theme's OWN 30px override that had been
keyed to the .form-group class name, since Bootstrap's bare .mb-3
only supplies 16px. No per-task review caught this because they all
verified the horizontal gutter but not vertical spacing against the
pre-upgrade baseline. Fixed (commit 03314cc): retargeted the theme's
_contact.scss rule to .mb-3, and discovered fixing it required
!important since Bootstrap 5 generates spacing utilities with
!important by design - a plain override silently loses. Re-review
independently confirmed on the live preview: all 4 form wrappers
compute 30px again, selector scope confirmed narrow (only 2 .mb-3
rules exist site-wide, ancestor-scoped correctly, no leak to other
pages), horizontal gutter unaffected, visual check clean.
Minor (non-blocking, unchanged from Task 2's finding): vendored
bootstrap-5.3.8/scss/tests/ (52KB, Bootstrap's own unit tests) is
inert dead weight - future cleanup candidate.
All 3 tasks + both discovered regressions resolved. Ready to merge.

content.text:
All three pieces of work are merged into `main` and verified:

- **Bootstrap 5.3 upgrade** (#7, `0b5abdb`) — including the two regressions caught mid-review: the résumé card stacking-context bug (missing `position: relative`) and the contact form's vertical spacing regression (`.form-group` → `.mb-3` losing the theme's 30px override).
- **Photo refresh + responsive blog images** (#8, `e620eb5`).
- **Rocket Loader fix + Bluesky footer link** (#9, `1b98d99`).

The `chore/bootstrap-5-upgrade` branch is already deleted locally and remotely, ledger confirms all 3 tasks plus both discovered regressions resolved and re-verified on the live preview. Working tree is clean aside from pre-existing untracked tooling artifacts (`.agent-traces/`, `.hugo_build.lock`, `.wrangler/`) that were already present before this session's work and aren't part of any pending task.

Nothing outstanding from this session — let me know if you want the production site double-checked for anything specific, or if there's new work to start.

Can we do a bit more image optimization?  I feel like there's more resizing to be done to shrink images at lower breakpoints.  Can you analyse the CSS breakpoints and work out if we can make smaller images that will perform faster?  (Also, you can use ~/battle.jpg as a new source for the youtube video thumbnail)  (Also also, why is hero-mask-svg served as a 50kb png file, while about-mask-svg is a 169byte svg?)

content.text:
Dug through the actual breakpoint math (Bootstrap 5's grid values, the theme's `desktop`/`tablet`/`mobile` mixins, and the `width: 90%/80%/45%` overrides in the SCSS) for all four responsive image spots. A few findings worth flagging before I change anything:

**The mask question first, since it's simple:** `hero-mask-svg.svg` already exists on disk (`static/images/hero/hero-mask-svg.svg`, 251 bytes) — it's the exact same rounded-rect-with-notch shape as `about-mask-svg.svg`, just mirrored. Nobody ever swapped the reference over; `hero.html` and `_single-blog-post.scss` still point at the 48KB PNG raster of the identical shape. That's a straight swap, no visual risk — I'll just do it.

**On "shrink images at lower breakpoints" — the data says it's actually the opposite in most spots.** Because the theme uses Bootstrap's grid (`col-lg-*`), these images go **full-width on mobile/tablet** and only shrink to a fraction of the container at `lg`+ (desktop). E.g. blog thumbnails render at ~266–374px on desktop (3-column grid) but 432–720px on phones/tablets (single column). So mobile isn't over-served by size — if anything desktop is, because we're still handing every ≥2x-DPR desktop browser the same 960px "2x" image regardless of the fact its column is often under 300px wide. That's the real waste: our `1x`/`2x` srcset only reacts to pixel density, not to the fact the box itself resizes across breakpoints.

**Bigger find: the blog post banner (`single.html`) has no 2x variant at all** — it's a single fixed 970px-wide image, but the container renders it up to ~1096px CSS-wide on large desktops. Any retina display is currently getting a blurry banner, sitewide, on every post.

**Proposed fix:** replace the `1x`/`2x` density srcset with proper width-descriptor srcset (`w`) + a `sizes` attribute matching the real column math per breakpoint, for all four spots (hero, about, blog thumb ×2, blog banner). That lets the browser pick correctly regardless of both viewport and DPR — genuinely smaller downloads at desktop, correctly sharp ones at retina/tablet, and fixes the banner blur bug as a side effect.

One honest caveat: a couple of source photos (`growth.jpg`, 815×666 native) are already being upscaled slightly to hit today's 960px "2x" tier — adding a bigger tier upscales them a touch further. Same category of pre-existing softness I flagged before, not new.

Switch responsive images to width-based srcset and add a video thumbnail

Blog thumbnails, hero image, and about image used density (1x/2x) srcset,
which picks purely on device pixel ratio and ignores the fact these
images render at very different CSS widths across Bootstrap breakpoints
(full-width single column below lg, 1/3-3/4 width above it). Replaced
with width-descriptor srcset plus a sizes attribute matching the actual
per-breakpoint column math, and added more width tiers so the browser
can pick a properly small file at each breakpoint instead of one of two
coarse options.

The blog post banner had no 2x variant at all and was serving a fixed
970px image into containers rendering up to ~1096px CSS-wide - retina
displays were getting a blurry banner on every post. Gave it the same
width-tier treatment.

Also:
- Swapped hero.html's mask reference from hero-mask-svg.png (48KB
  raster) to hero-mask-svg.svg (251 bytes) - an SVG of the identical
  shape already sat unused next to it, just never wired up.
- Replaced the hero video-popup thumbnail source with battle.jpg,
  moved into the Hugo asset pipeline with a proper 1x/2x/3x srcset
  (this one has a fixed CSS box size regardless of breakpoint, so
  density-based srcset is the right tool here).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

content.text:
Ran the actual numbers (required upscale factor per source vs. every generated tier) rather than going from memory:

**Genuinely need a bigger source (upscale on the big banner tier, and two of them even bleed into the 1200px thumb tier):**

| Post | Image | Native size | Thumb 1200w | Banner 2000w |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| The geometry of software teams | `geometry.jpg` | 1440×1121 | upscales (1.24×) | upscales (1.39×) |
| Taking Charge of Your Career Growth | `growth.jpg` | 1558×1116 | upscales (1.24×) | upscales (1.28×) |
| Increase your hiring success with job success profiles | `handshake.jpg` (currently `interview.jpg`) | 1403×1346 | upscales (1.03×, barely) | upscales (1.43×) |

**Fine at the thumb tier, but marginal on the banner's biggest tier:**

| Post | Image | Native size | Banner 2000w |
|---|---|---|---|
| Estimating your way to success | `writing.jpg` | 1687×2519 | upscales (1.19×) |

**Plenty of headroom everywhere (no replacement needed):** `filter.jpg` (2272×1408), `hypothesis.jpg` (2456×2226).

If you're hunting for replacements, `geometry.jpg` and `handshake`/`interview.jpg` are the two worth prioritizing — they're short on both dimensions relative to what a full-bleed banner needs. As a rule of thumb, anything **at least ~2000px on the shorter side** clears every tier we generate (thumb + banner) with margin.
OK, I've replaced geometry, growth, interview and writing jpegs in my home directory with images that are at least 2000px wide.

content.text: Confirmed — the "Left" anchor centers vertically, chopping the top of his head (and the table legs at the bottom don't matter). Let me check the thumbnail crop too, since both use the same anchor field.

The banner's aspect ratio forces heavy vertical cropping, and "Left" only biases horizontally (vertical defaults to centered) — that's what's slicing off the top of his head. Good news: the thumbnail crop needs *zero* vertical cropping at all (height already matches exactly), so switching the anchor to `TopLeft` will fix the banner without touching the thumbnail.

content.text: PR #11 opened: https://github.com/rodbegbie/begbie-hugo/pull/11 (the "2 uncommitted changes" warning is again just the pre-existing untracked `.agent-traces/`/`.wrangler/` artifacts, unrelated). `sitemap.xml` was already automatic and correct; `robots.txt` is new and allow-all, pointing at the sitemap.
Back to the image optimisation branch.  Are you able to set imaging.webp hint value on the images?  The about and hero image should be "picture" and the blog images are all "drawing".

input.query: image processing options webp hint quality anchor Resize Fill

content.text: Good — `hint` is a per-operation token in Hugo's processing spec string (WebP-only preset: `drawing`, `icon`, `photo`, `picture`, `text`), so I can set it independently on each `Resize`/`Fill` call. One thing before I do this across the board:

questions.question: The hero video-popup thumbnail (battle.jpg) isn't the hero/about portrait or a blog illustration — it's a real photo. What hint should it get?

options.label: photo (Recommended)
options.description: libwebp's preset for outdoor/natural-light photographs, which is what battle.jpg is.

options.label: picture
options.description: Same as hero/about — treat it as consistent with the other portrait-style images.

options.label: Leave unset
options.description: Don't set a hint on this one, only on the images you explicitly named.
